title: Understanding Latent Correlation-Based Multiview Learning and Self-Supervision: An Identifiability Perspective

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## UNDERSTANDING LATENT CORRELATION-BASED MULTIVIEW LEARNING AND SELF-SUPERVISION: AN IDENTIFIABILITY PERSPECTIVE
**Qi Lyu Xiao Fu** _[∗]_ **Weiran Wang Songtao Lu** School of EECS School of EECS Google Inc. IBM Research Oregon State Univ. Oregon State Univ. Mountain View Yorktown Heights

## ABSTRACT

Multiple views of data, both naturally acquired (e.g., image and audio) and artificially produced (e.g., via adding different noise to data samples), have proven useful in enhancing representation learning. Natural views are often handled by multiview analysis tools, e.g., (deep) canonical correlation analysis [(D)CCA], while the artificial ones are frequently used in self-supervised learning (SSL) paradigms, e.g., BYOL and Barlow Twins. Both types of approaches often involve learning neural feature extractors such that the embeddings of data exhibit high cross-view correlations. Although intuitive, the effectiveness of correlationbased neural embedding is mostly empirically validated. This work aims to understand latent correlation maximization-based deep multiview learning from a latent component identification viewpoint. An intuitive generative model of multiview data is adopted, where the views are different nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components. Since the shared components are view/distortion-invariant, representing the data using such components is believed to reveal the identity of the samples effectively and robustly. Under this model, latent correlation maximization is shown to guarantee the extraction of the shared components across views (up to certain ambiguities). In addition, it is further shown that the private information in each view can be provably disentangled from the shared using proper regularization design. A finite sample analysis, which has been rare in nonlinear mixture identifiability study, is also presented. The theoretical results and newly designed regularization are tested on a series of tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

One pillar of unsupervised representation learning is multiview learning. Extracting shared information from multiple “views” (e.g., image and audio) of data entities has been considered a major means to fend against noise and data scarcity. A key computational tool for multiview learning is _canonical correlation analysis_ (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936). The classic CCA seeks linear transformation matrices such that transformed views are maximally correlated. A number of works studied nonlinear extensions of CCA; see kernel CCA in (Lai & Fyfe, 2000) and _deep learning-based CCA_ (DCCA) in (Andrew et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2015). DCCA and its variants were shown to largely outperform the classical linear CCA in many tasks.

In recent years, a series of _self-supervised learning_ (SSL) paradigms were proposed. These SSL approaches exhibit a lot of similarities with DCCA approaches, except that the “views” are noisy data “augmenented” from the original clean data. To be specific, different views are generated by distorting data—e.g., using rotating, cropping, and/or adding noise to data samples (Dosovitskiy et al., 2015; Gidaris et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Then, neural encoders are employed to map these artificial views to embeddings that are highly correlated across views. This genre—which will be referred to as artificial multiview SSL (AM-SSL)—includes some empirically successful frameworks, e.g., BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021).

Notably, many DCCA and AM-SSL approaches involve (explicitly or implicitly) searching for highly correlated representations from multiple views, using neural feature extractors (encoders). The empirical success of DCCA and AM-SSL bears an important research question: _How to understand the role of cross-view correlation in deep multiview learning?_ Furthermore, how to use such understanding to design theory-backed learning criteria to serve various purposes?

Intuitively, it makes sense that many DCCA and AM-SSL paradigms involve latent correlation maximization in their loss functions, as such loss functions lead to similar/identical representations from different views—which identifies view-invariant essential information that is often identityrevealing. However, beyond intuition, theoretical support of latent correlation-based deep multiview learning had been less studied, until recent works started exploring this direction in both nonlinear CCA and AM-SSL (see, e.g., (Lyu & Fu, 2020; Von K¨ugelgen et al., 2021; Zimmermann et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2021; Saunshi et al., 2019; Tosh et al., 2021)), but more insights and theoretical underpinnings remain to be discovered under more realistic and challenging settings. In this work, we offer an understanding to the role of latent correlation maximization that is seen in a number of DCCA and AM-SSL systems from a nonlinear mixture learning viewpoint—and use such understanding to assist various learning tasks, e.g., clustering, cross-view translation, and cross-sample generation. Our detailed contributions are:

**(i) Understanding Latent Correlation Maximization - Shared Component Identification.** We start with a concept that has been advocated in many multiview learning works. In particular, the views are nonlinear mixtures of shared and private latent components; see, e.g., (Huang et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016). The shared components are distortion/view invariant and identity-revealing. The private components and view-specific nonlinear mixing processes determine the different appearances of the views. By assuming independence between the shared and private components and invertibility of the data generating process, we show that maximizing the correlation of latent representations extracted from different views leads to identification of the ground-truth shared components up to invertible transformations.

**(ii) Imposing Additional Constraints - Private Component Identification.** Using the understanding to latent correlation maximization-type loss functions in DCCA and AM-SSL, we take a step further. We show that with carefully imposed constraints, the private components in the views can also be identified, under reasonable assumptions. Learning private components can facilitate tasks such as cross-view and cross-sample data generation (Huang et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2018).

**(iii) Finite-Sample Analysis.** Most existing unsupervised nonlinear mixture identification works, e.g., those from the nonlinear independent component analysis (ICA) literature (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016; 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020; Locatello et al., 2020; Gresele et al., 2020), are based on infinite data. This is perhaps because finite sample analysis for unsupervised learning is generally much more challenging relative to supervised cases—and there is no existing “universal” analytical tools. In this work, we provide sample complexity analysis for the proposed _unsupervised_ multiview learning criterion. We come up with a success metric for characterizing the performance of latent component extraction, and integrate generalization analysis and numerical differentiation to quantify this metric. To our best knowledge, this is the first finite-sample analysis of nonlinear mixture model-based multiview unsupervised learning.

**(iv) Practical Implementation.** Based on the theoretical understanding, we propose a latent correlation-maximization based multiview learning criterion for extracting both the shared components and private components. To realize the criterion, a notable innovation is a minimax neural regularizer that serves for extracting the private components. The regularizer shares the same purpose of some known independence promoters (e.g., _Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion_ (HSIC) (Gretton et al., 2007)) but is arguably easier to implement using stochastic gradient algorithms.

**Notation.** The notations used in this work are summarized in the supplementary material.

## 2.2 LATENT CORRELATION MAXIMIZATION/COMPONENT MATCHING IN AM-SSL

Similar to DCCA, the goal of AM-SSL is also to find identity-revealing embeddings of data samples without using labels. The idea is often realized via intentionally distorting the data to create multiple artificial views. Then, the encoders are require to produce highly correlated (or closely matched) embeddings from such views. In AM-SSL, the views _**x**_[(1)] and _**x**_[(2)] are different augmentations (e.g., by adding noise, cropping, and rotation) of the sample _**x**_ .

_•_ **Barlow Twins.** The most recent development, namely, the Barlow Twins network (Zbontar et al., 2021) is appealing since it entails a succinct implementation. ## 3 UNDERSTANDING LATENT CORRELATION MAXIMIZATION

In this section, we offer understandings to latent correlation maximization (and latent component matching) from an unsupervised nonlinear multiview mixture identification viewpoint. We will also show that such understanding can help improve multiview learning criteria to serve different purposes, e.g., cross-view and cross-sample data generation.

Assumption 1 is considered reasonable under both AM-SSL and DCCA settings. For AM-SSL, the private information can be understood as random data augmentation noise-induced components, and thus it makes sense to assume that such noise is independent with the shared information (which corresponds to the identity-revealing components of the data sample). In DCCA problems, the private style information can change drastically from view to view (e.g., audio, text, video) without changing the shared content information (e.g., identity of the entity)—which also shows independence between the two parts. In our analysis, we will assume that _D_ and _Dq_ are known to facilitate exposition. In practice, these parameters are often selected using a validation set.

**Learning Goals.** Our interest lies in extracting _**z** ℓ_ and _**c**_[(] _ℓ[q]_[)] (up to certain ambiguities) from the views in an unsupervised manner. In particular, we hope to answer under what conditions these latent components can be identified—and to what extent. As mentioned, _**z** ℓ_ is view/distortioninvariant and thus should be identity-revealing. The ability of extracting it may explain DCCA and AM-SSL’s effectiveness. In addition, the identification of _**c**_[(] _ℓ[q]_[)] and the mixing processes may help generate data in different views.

## 4 IMPLEMENTATION

**Enforcing Group Statistical Independence.** A notable challenge is the statistical independence constraint in (7d), whose enforcement is often an art. Early methods such as (Taleb & Jutten, 1999; Hyv¨arinen & Oja, 2000) may be costly. The HSIC method in (Gretton et al., 2007) which measures the correlation of two variables in a kernel space can be used in our framework, but kernels sometimes induce large memory overheads and are sensitive to parameter (e.g., kernel width) selection.

## 5 RELATED WORK - NONLINEAR ICA AND LATENT DISENTANGLEMENT

Other than the DCCA and AM-SSL works, our design for private and shared information separation also draws insights from two topics in unsupervised representation learning, namely, nonlinear ICA (nICA) (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016; 2017; Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020) and latent factor disentanglement (Higgins et al., 2017; Kim & Mnih, 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019; Lopez et al., 2018), which are recently offered a unifying perspective in (Khemakhem et al., 2020). The nICA works aim to _separate_ nonlinearly mixed latent components to an individual component level, which is in general impossible unless additional information associated with each sample (e.g., time frame labels (Hyvarinen & Morioka, 2016) and class labels (Hyvarinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020)) is used. Multiple views are less studied in the context of nICA, with the recent exception in (Locatello et al., 2020) and (Gresele et al., 2020). Nonetheless, their models are different from ours and the approaches cannot extract the private information from views with different nonlinear models. This is similar to the data augmentation ideas in AM-SSL. Using this multiview data, we apply different multiview learning paradigms that match the latent representations (or maximize the correlations of learned representations) across views. ## 6.2 VALIDATING THEOREM 2 - SHARED AND PRIVATE COMPONENT DISENTANGLEMENT

We use data generation examples to support our claim in Theorem 2—i.e., with our designed regularizer _R_ in (10), one can provably disentangle the shared and private latent components.

**Cars3D Data for Cross-sample Data Generation.** We use the Cars3D dataset (Reed et al., 2015) that contains different car CAD models. For each car image, there are three defining aspects (namely, ‘type’, ‘elevation’ and ‘azimuth’).

## 7 CONCLUSION

In this work, we provided theoretical understandings to the role of latent correlation maximization (latent component matching) that is often used in DCCA and AM-SSL methods from an unsupervised nonlinear mixture learning viewpoint. In particular, we modeled multiview data as nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components, and showed that latent correlation maximization ensures to extract the shared components—which are believed to be identity-revealing. In addition, we showed that, with a carefully designed constraint (which is approximated by a neural regularizer), one can further disentangle the shared and private information, under reasonable conditions. We also analyzed the sample complexity for extracting the shared information, which has not been addressed in nonlinear component analysis works, to our best knowledge. To realize our learning criterion, we proposed a slack variable-assisted latent correlation maximization approach, with a novel minimax neural regularizer for promoting group independence. We tested our method over synthetic and real data. The results corroborated our design goals and theoretical analyses.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper provides a theoretical analysis of latent correlation-based multiview learning (DCCA/SSL) by modeling data as nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components. It proves that maximizing latent correlation identifies shared components and shows that specific regularization can disentangle private information, supported by a finite-sample analysis.

The paper makes a strong theoretical contribution by bridging the gap between empirical SSL methods and identifiability theory in nonlinear ICA. The inclusion of finite-sample complexity bounds is particularly impressive and rare in this domain. The work successfully explains why correlation-based objectives are effective for representation learning.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Role of Invertibility in Theorem 1: Theorem 1 claims that latent correlation maximization identifies shared components up to an invertible transformation, but the proof relies on the assumption that the generative functions are invertible. In practice, neural network encoders used in DCCA and Barlow Twins are rarely strictly invertible, and the paper does not address the impact of non-invertible mappings on the identifiability result. If the mapping is not invertible, the shared components may be collapsed or lost in the latent space. The authors should clarify whether the result holds for non-invertible encoders or explicitly state the conditions under which the learned representation remains faithful to the ground-truth shared components.

- Gap between Population and Empirical Objectives: The theoretical analysis in Section 3.3 assumes the population case (N=infinity), while the implementation in Section 4 introduces slack variables and reconstruction losses to handle the empirical objective. There is a disconnect between the theoretical guarantee for the population objective and the actual algorithm used in experiments. Specifically, the reconstruction loss (V) is used to promote invertibility, yet the theoretical theorems do not explicitly model the influence of this reconstruction error on the identifiability of the shared components. A formal link between the reconstruction error and the bound on the identification error would significantly strengthen the paper's claims.

- Dependence on Strong Independence Assumptions: Assumption 1 requires the shared and private components to be statistically independent. While this is a standard assumption in ICA, it is quite restrictive for real-world multiview data where private noise and shared content might be correlated (e.g., lighting conditions in an image often depend on the object's geometry). The paper does not explore the robustness of the identifiability result when this independence assumption is violated. A brief discussion or a synthetic experiment showing the degradation of performance when the independence assumption is relaxed would provide a more realistic assessment of the method's utility.

- Complexity of the Minimax Regularizer: The proposed minimax regularizer for enforcing group independence (Equation 9) introduces a new optimization challenge. The paper suggests using neural networks to represent the functions in the supremum, but this turns the training process into a complex game that may be unstable. The paper lacks a convergence analysis for this specific minimax objective, which is critical given the known instability of GAN-like training. Providing empirical evidence of the training stability or a discussion on the choice of hyperparameters for the regularizer would be essential for practitioners.

- Tightness of Finite Sample Bounds: Theorem 3 provides a finite-sample complexity bound, but the dependence on the Rademacher complexity and the function mismatch parameter (nu) is quite abstract. It remains unclear if these bounds are tight or if they are overly pessimistic for standard neural network architectures. The paper would benefit from a more concrete example or a numerical simulation that tracks the error bound as a function of sample size to verify if the theoretical rate matches the empirical convergence observed in the experiments.

- Empirical verification of the finite-sample bound rate: Theorem 3 provides a theoretical finite-sample complexity bound of O(N^{-1/3}). While this is a novel contribution, the paper does not empirically validate whether this specific rate is observed in practice. The authors should include a controlled simulation on a synthetic dataset—such as a simple 2D nonlinear mixture model—where they systematically vary the sample size N and plot the identification error metric (the left-hand side of Equation 8) against N on a log-log scale. This would demonstrate that the theoretical bound is not just a loose upper limit but captures the actual convergence behavior of the proposed learning criterion.

- Robustness analysis under violation of group independence: Assumption 1 requires strict statistical independence between shared and private components, yet the paper does not investigate the sensitivity of the proposed estimator when this condition is violated. Real-world multiview data often exhibits correlations between content and style/noise, such as lighting intensity depending on the object's geometry. To demonstrate the practical utility of the method, the authors should perform an experiment using a controlled synthetic DGP where they introduce a tunable correlation parameter between the shared and private components. Tracking the degradation of the disentanglement performance as this correlation increases would provide a necessary boundary for the method's applicability.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the impact of non-invertible encoders on Theorem 1 and discuss how the reconstruction loss in the implementation helps bridge this gap.

- Provide a more detailed discussion on the stability of the minimax regularizer and include a brief analysis or empirical check on its convergence.

- Explicitly address the limitations of the independence assumption (Assumption 1) and its potential impact on real-world data where shared and private components may be correlated.

- Include a sensitivity analysis or a plot showing the convergence of the proposed method relative to the theoretical sample complexity bound in Theorem 3.

comments.title: Inaccuracy regarding the novelty of finite-sample analysis
comments.quote: To our best knowledge, this is the first finite-sample analysis of nonlinear mixture model-based multiview unsupervised learning.
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by existing literature. For instance, Fu et al. (2022) provide finite-sample guarantees for DeepCCA-based identification of shared information under post-nonlinear models. Since your work focuses on latent correlation maximization—a framework that encompasses DCCA—this claim overlooks established results. Please revise this statement to accurately acknowledge existing finite-sample analyses for related nonlinear multiview models.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding private information extraction in prior work
comments.quote: In addition, our learning criterion is able to extract the private information, while the work in (Von Kugelgen et al., 2021) did not consider this aspect.
comments.explanation: The claim that Von Kügelgen et al. (2021) did not consider private information extraction is incorrect. Their paper, 'Self-Supervised Learning with Data Augmentations Provably Isolates Content from Style,' is explicitly centered on the disentanglement of content (shared) and style (private) information. Please correct this misrepresentation of their core contribution.

comments.title: Dimensionality constraint for invertibility
comments.quote: In addition, $\bm{g}^{(q)}(\cdot):\mathbb{R}^{D+D_{q}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{M_{q}}$ is an *invertible* and *smooth* nonlinear transformation, which is unknown.
comments.explanation: The definition of $\bm{g}^{(q)}$ as an invertible mapping between $\mathbb{R}^{D+D_q}$ and $\mathbb{R}^{M_q}$ requires that the domain and codomain have the same dimension. If $M_q \neq D + D_q$, the function cannot be a bijection between these Euclidean spaces. Please explicitly state the assumption $M_q = D + D_q$ to ensure the invertibility assumption is mathematically well-posed.

comments.quote: we evaluate the learned  $\tilde{f}^{(q)}$ 's by combining  $\tilde{z}_{\ell}^{(q)} = \tilde{f}_{\mathrm{S}}^{(q)}(\pmb{x}_{\ell}^{(q)})$  and  $\tilde{c}_j^{(q)} = \tilde{f}_{\mathrm{P}}^{(q)}(\pmb{x}_j^{(q)})$  and generating  $\tilde{\pmb{x}}_{\ell,j}^{(q)} = \tilde{\pmb{r}}^{(q)}([(\tilde{\pmb{z}}_{\ell}^{(q)})^{\top}, (\pmb{c}_j^{(q)})^{\top}]^{\top})$

(10), it fails to define the reconstruction loss (e.g., $L_{rec} = \|x - r(f(x))\|^2$) that anchors the latent space to the data. Please define this loss function explicitly to clarify how the latent space retains sufficient information for reconstruction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the scope of identifiability guarantees
comments.quote: Under this model, latent correlation maximization is shown to guarantee the extraction of the shared components across views (up to certain ambiguities).
comments.explanation: The abstract claims guaranteed extraction but does not specify the nature of the 'ambiguities.' In nonlinear ICA literature, these are typically restricted to specific classes of transformations, such as component-wise invertible functions or permutations. Please clarify the class of transformations to align the abstract with the formal results in Section 3.

comments.title: Equivalence of Slack Variable Formulation to DCCA
comments.quote: The slack variable $\bm{g}$ represents the common latent embedding learned from the two views. Conceptually, this is also latent correlation maximization (or latent component matching).
comments.explanation: The equivalence between the slack variable formulation in (3) and standard DCCA is not immediate. Minimizing the objective with respect to $\bm{g}$ yields $\bm{g} = \frac{1}{2}(\bm{f}^{(1)} + \bm{f}^{(2)})$, and the constraints on $\bm{g}$ do not automatically imply $\mathbb{E}[(\bm{f}^{(q)}_i)^2] = 1$. Please add a remark clarifying that this equivalence assumes the whitening constraints $\mathbb{E}[\bm{f}^{(q)}\bm{f}^{(q)\top}] = \bm{I}$ are enforced by the architecture.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper provides a theoretical framework linking latent correlation maximization in deep multiview learning and self-supervised learning to identifiability of shared and private latent components under a nonlinear mixture model. The theoretical results (Theorems 1 and 2) are clearly stated and appear technically sound under the stated assumptions. The finite-sample analysis (Theorem 3) is a notable contribution, as such analysis is rare in nonlinear mixture identifiability. However, several assumptions are strong and may limit practical applicability, and the empirical validation is somewhat thin, relying primarily on synthetic and controlled datasets with limited comparison to relevant baselines. The paper makes a meaningful contribution but would benefit from addressing these limitations more explicitly.

Overview issues:

- Generative model assumptions may not hold for real-world multiview data: The generative model (5) assumes that views are generated from a common set of shared components plus view-specific private components, with group independence (Assumption 1). While this is plausible for some settings (e.g., content-style separation), it is questionable for many real-world multiview pairs such as image and audio, where the semantic granularity of shared information may differ (e.g., image captures visual appearance, audio captures sound, and the 'shared' identity may be at different abstraction levels). The paper acknowledges this briefly but does not critically assess the realism or discuss when the model might fail. The authors should add a discussion of scenarios where the generative model is violated (e.g., views with different semantic granularity, misaligned views) and how such violations affect the identifiability guarantees. This would help readers understand the scope of applicability.

- Invertibility and smoothness assumptions are strong for deep networks: The theoretical results assume that the mixing functions g^{(q)} and the learned encoders f^{(q)} are invertible and smooth (third-order differentiable). In practice, many deep neural networks use non-invertible layers (e.g., ReLU, pooling, dropout), which violate these assumptions. The paper uses a reconstruction loss to encourage invertibility, but this is a soft constraint and does not guarantee invertibility, especially for high-dimensional data where reconstruction may be imperfect. The authors should discuss the gap between the theoretical requirement and practical implementation, and ideally provide empirical evidence (e.g., reconstruction error or invertibility metrics) that the learned encoders approximately satisfy invertibility. Without this, the theoretical guarantees may not apply to the actual learned representations.

- Finite-sample analysis has limited practical guidance: Theorem 3 provides a sample complexity bound of O(N^{-1/3}) for the disentanglement metric, which is a valuable theoretical contribution. However, the bound depends on several quantities (Rademacher complexity, approximation error ν, constants C_d, C_f, C_p) that are not quantified in practice. The paper does not discuss what sample sizes are needed for reliable identification in typical settings (e.g., for image data with D=10, D_q=20). Additionally, the bound is for the population expectation of a derivative-based metric, not for the actual recovery error of the shared or private components. The authors should provide a more interpretable discussion of the bound, perhaps with a numerical example or simulation showing how the bound behaves with realistic parameter values. This would make the finite-sample analysis more useful for practitioners.

- Empirical validation is limited to controlled datasets with weak baselines: The experiments validate Theorems 1 and 2 on synthetic data, multiview MNIST, Cars3D, dSprites, and MNIST (supplementary). While these datasets are useful for proof-of-concept, they are relatively simple and controlled. The paper does not test on more complex, high-dimensional real-world multiview data (e.g., image+audio, video+text) where the generative model assumptions may be strained. Furthermore, the baselines for shared component extraction (DCCA, Barlow Twins, BYOL) are compared only on clustering accuracy, which is a downstream metric that does not directly measure identifiability of the true shared components. For private component disentanglement, the paper uses cross-sample generation but only compares with an ablation (without regularizer R), not with other disentanglement methods (e.g., VAE-based methods, nonlinear ICA). The authors should include comparisons with relevant baselines for disentanglement (e.g., β-VAE, FactorVAE, or the method of von Kügelgen et al. 2021) and ideally test on a real-world multiview dataset (e.g., YouTube-8M, NYUv2) to demonstrate practical relevance.

- The independence regularizer R may not guarantee group independence in practice: Proposition 1 states that if sup_{φ,τ} R^{(q)} = 0 over all measurable functions, then the components are independent. In practice, the regularizer uses neural networks φ and τ, which are function approximators, so the supremum is not achieved exactly. The paper does not analyze how the approximation error in the regularizer affects the disentanglement guarantees. Additionally, the minimax optimization (10) may be difficult to train stably, and the paper does not discuss convergence or sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., λ). The authors should provide an analysis of the approximation error or at least empirical evidence (e.g., independence tests like HSIC) that the learned representations satisfy approximate independence. Without this, the claim of provable disentanglement may be overstated.

- Theoretical claims about private component identification require stronger justification: Theorem 2 states that under the same conditions as Theorem 1 plus the independence constraint (7d), the learned private components satisfy c̃^{(q)} = δ^{(q)}(c^{(q)}) for an invertible δ^{(q)}. However, the proof (in supplementary) likely relies on the invertibility of the overall mapping f^{(q)}∘g^{(q)} and the independence between shared and private components. A subtle issue is that the private components c^{(q)} may not be identifiable up to invertible transformation if the mixing functions g^{(q)} are not sufficiently diverse or if the private components have non-generic distributions (e.g., Gaussian). The paper should explicitly discuss the conditions under which private component identification is possible (e.g., non-Gaussianity of c^{(q)}, or additional assumptions like those in nonlinear ICA). This is a known challenge in the field, and the paper should address it to avoid overclaiming.

- No synthetic experiment validating finite-sample bound: Theorem 3 provides a sample complexity bound, but the paper never demonstrates that this bound is meaningful by constructing a synthetic DGP where the bound can be evaluated. Without a controlled experiment that varies N and measures the left-hand side of (8), readers cannot calibrate how large N needs to be for the bound to be non-vacuous. The authors should generate data from a known nonlinear mixture (e.g., using a small invertible neural network for g^{(q)}), run the proposed method for increasing N, and plot the empirical metric against the O(N^{-1/3}) rate. This would ground the theoretical rate and show whether the constants are practically relevant.

- Missing comparison with nonlinear ICA and VAE disentanglement baselines: The paper claims to extract private components but compares only against an ablation without the regularizer. For private component identification, the relevant baselines include nonlinear ICA methods (e.g., iVAE, SlowVAE) and VAE-based disentanglement methods (e.g., β-VAE, FactorVAE). Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether the proposed regularizer offers any advantage over existing approaches. The authors should apply these baselines to the Cars3D and dSprites tasks, measuring shared/private recovery via the same cross-sample generation metric, and report quantitative scores (e.g., disentanglement metrics like MIG or DCI).

- No discussion of how to choose D and D_q in practice: The paper states that D and D_q are 'often selected using a validation set' but provides no guidance on what validation criterion to use or how sensitive the identifiability guarantees are to misspecification of these dimensions. In real applications, the true latent dimensions are unknown, and over- or under-estimating them could break the theoretical guarantees. The authors should either propose a principled method for selecting these dimensions (e.g., based on reconstruction error or cross-validation of downstream tasks) or analyze how the bounds in Theorem 3 degrade when D or D_q are mis-specified.

- Practical invertibility of learned encoders is not assessed: The theory requires invertible f^{(q)} and g^{(q)}, but the implementation uses a reconstruction loss as a soft constraint. The paper does not report reconstruction errors or any invertibility metric (e.g., condition number of Jacobians) for the learned encoders on real data. Without such evidence, readers cannot assess whether the theoretical guarantees apply to the actual learned representations. The authors should compute and report the average reconstruction error and, if feasible, the approximate invertibility (e.g., via the Jacobian determinant or a round-trip error) for the MNIST and Cars3D experiments.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a critical discussion of the generative model's realism for real-world multiview data, including scenarios where assumptions are violated and how that affects guarantees.

- Provide empirical evidence that the learned encoders approximately satisfy invertibility (e.g., reconstruction error, invertibility metrics) and discuss the gap between theory and practice.

- Include comparisons with relevant disentanglement baselines (e.g., β-VAE, FactorVAE, von Kügelgen et al. 2021) on at least one real-world multiview dataset to demonstrate practical utility.

- Analyze the approximation error of the independence regularizer and its impact on disentanglement guarantees, or provide empirical independence tests (e.g., HSIC) on learned representations.

- Clarify the conditions for private component identifiability (e.g., non-Gaussianity, diversity of mixing functions) and discuss limitations of Theorem 2.

comments.title: Invertibility constraint is ambiguous for non-square maps
comments.quote: subject to $\bm{f}^{(q)}$ for $q=1,2$ are invertible, (7b)
comments.explanation: The functions f^{(q)}: ℝ^{M_q} → ℝ^{D+D_q} map between spaces of possibly different dimensions. Standard invertibility requires equal dimensions (M_q = D+D_q). The paper later uses a reconstruction loss to encourage invertibility, but the constraint as stated is mathematically imprecise. The authors should clarify that they require the existence of a left-inverse (i.e., f^{(q)} is injective and there exists a measurable right-inverse) or specify that M_q = D+D_q is assumed. Rewrite (7b) as: 'f^{(q)} is injective and admits a measurable left-inverse' or state the dimension assumption explicitly.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about regularizer ease of implementation
comments.quote:
components. The regularizer shares the same purpose of some known independence promoters (e.g., *Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion* (HSIC) *(Gretton et al., 2007)*) but is arguably easier to implement using stochastic gradient algorithms.

Not
comments.explanation: The paper claims the proposed minimax regularizer is 'arguably easier to implement' than HSIC, but provides no justification or comparison. HSIC can be computed in closed form with a kernel choice (e.g., RBF) and optimized via standard gradient descent, while the minimax formulation requires alternating optimization between two neural networks, which is often harder to tune and more computationally expensive. Without analysis or empirical evidence, this claim is unsupported. The authors should either provide a concrete comparison (e.g., runtime, convergence) or remove the claim.

comments.title: Overclaiming sample complexity novelty
comments.quote: We also analyzed the sample complexity for extracting the shared information, which has not been addressed in nonlinear component analysis works, to our best knowledge.
comments.explanation: The claim that sample complexity for extracting shared information has 'not been addressed in nonlinear component analysis works' is too broad and may be inaccurate. Prior work on nonlinear ICA (e.g., Hyvärinen et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020) and identifiable VAE (e.g., iVAE) includes finite-sample analyses or discussions of sample complexity for recovering latent components under certain assumptions. The authors should either cite specific works that address sample complexity for shared component extraction in nonlinear mixtures, or qualify the claim by specifying the exact setting (e.g., 'to our best knowledge, no prior work has provided finite-sample guarantees for shared component extraction under the specific generative model and correlation-based criterion considered here').

comments.title: Overstated guarantee of shared component extraction in abstract
comments.quote: Under this model, latent correlation maximization is shown to guarantee the extraction of the shared components across views (up to certain ambiguities).
comments.explanation: The abstract states that latent correlation maximization 'guarantees' extraction of shared components, but the guarantee is qualified only by 'up to certain ambiguities' without specifying what those ambiguities are. The paper's Theorem 1 shows that the extracted shared component is an invertible transformation of the true shared component, which is a nontrivial ambiguity (invertible function). The abstract should explicitly mention this ambiguity (e.g., 'up to an invertible transformation') to avoid misleading readers into thinking the components are recovered exactly. The current phrasing could be interpreted as a stronger claim than what is proven.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of 'proper regularization design' in abstract
comments.quote: In addition, it is further shown that the private information in each view can be provably disentangled from the shared using proper regularization design.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'proper regularization design' is vague and does not specify what the regularization is or how it achieves disentanglement. The abstract should briefly indicate the nature of the regularization (e.g., an independence-promoting regularizer based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion or a similar measure) to give readers a concrete sense of the contribution. Without this, the claim of provable disentanglement appears underspecified. The authors should add a short description of the regularizer, such as 'using a regularizer that enforces independence between the extracted shared and private components.'

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper offers a theoretical lens on latent correlation maximization for multiview learning and self-supervision, deriving identifiability guarantees for shared and private components under a nonlinear mixture model. The finite-sample analysis and minimax independence regularizer are ambitious contributions, but several gaps between theoretical assumptions and practical implementation weaken the overall argument. The empirical validation relies heavily on qualitative examples and lacks standard quantitative disentanglement metrics.

This work makes a valuable attempt to bridge nonlinear identifiability theory with practical multiview and SSL objectives. The generative model framing is intuitive, and the extension to finite-sample bounds is a notable step forward for the field. The main weakness lies in the disconnect between the strict theoretical assumptions and the approximate optimization strategies used in practice, as well as the reliance on qualitative experiments to support disentanglement claims.

Overview issues:

- Gap between theoretical invertibility and practical implementation: The theory assumes exact invertibility of the mixing and encoding functions (Section 3.1, Assumption 1; Section 3.2, Eq 7b). The implementation relies on a reconstruction loss to approximate this property (Section 4, Eq 10). Reconstruction error only guarantees approximate invertibility on the training distribution and does not prevent the network from learning non-invertible mappings that still minimize the loss. This gap matters because Theorems 1 and 2 explicitly require invertibility to establish the functional relationship between the observed data and the latent components. Without it, the theoretical guarantees no longer apply to the trained models. I suggest incorporating an explicit invertibility constraint, such as flow-based architectures or Jacobian regularization, and analyzing how the approximation error propagates through the identifiability bounds.

- Instability and lack of analysis for the independence regularizer: Theorem 2 and Equation 9 depend on enforcing exact statistical independence between the extracted shared and private components via a minimax objective. The paper proposes using neural networks to approximate this constraint but provides no analysis of the optimization dynamics or stability of this objective. Minimax independence regularizers are notoriously unstable and often suffer from mode collapse or gradient vanishing. This matters because the regularizer directly competes with the correlation maximization objective, and uncontrolled instability could degrade the quality of the shared representations. I recommend adding an ablation study on the regularization weight $ eta$, reporting gradient norms during training, and discussing failure modes where the independence term dominates or fails to converge.

- Unjustified sample complexity rate and metric interpretation: Theorem 3 presents a finite-sample bound of $O((	ext{complexity})^{2/3})$ for the expected squared partial derivatives of the shared encoder with respect to the private components. The exponent $2/3$ is non-standard and appears without derivation in the main text. More importantly, the theorem bounds a derivative metric rather than the distance to the true latent space. A small derivative bound does not directly translate to accurate latent recovery, especially given the inherent permutation and scaling ambiguities in correlation maximization. This matters because the main contribution claims a novel finite-sample analysis, but the bound's tightness and practical relevance remain unclear. I suggest clarifying the derivation of the rate, explicitly linking the derivative metric to latent recovery error, and including a simulation that demonstrates how sample size affects actual disentanglement quality.

- Overstated novelty regarding finite-sample analysis: The introduction claims this work provides the first finite-sample analysis for nonlinear mixture multiview learning. The literature context explicitly lists a 2022 paper by the same authors on finite-sample analysis of DeepCCA. This direct contradiction weakens the novelty claim and raises questions about the paper's positioning. It matters because reviewers and readers will expect a clear distinction between this work and the prior analysis. I recommend acknowledging the prior work, detailing the specific technical differences in model assumptions or bounding techniques, and revising the novelty statement to accurately reflect the contribution.

- Insufficient quantitative evaluation of disentanglement: The experimental validation of Theorem 2 relies heavily on qualitative cross-sample generation on the Cars3D dataset. The paper lacks standard quantitative disentanglement metrics to objectively measure how well the shared and private components are separated. Without these metrics, it is impossible to assess whether the proposed regularizer actually improves disentanglement compared to baselines or if the visual results are merely artifacts of the generation pipeline. This matters because the theoretical claims hinge on provable disentanglement, and qualitative evidence alone is insufficient for a top venue. I suggest adding established metrics like Mutual Information Gap or Spectral Angle, comparing against independence-promoting baselines, and reporting statistical significance across multiple runs.

- Concrete parametric example for the sample complexity bound: Theorem 3 provides an abstract finite-sample bound involving Rademacher complexity and function mismatch, but the paper never computes this bound for a specific, fully-specified model. Readers cannot gauge how the bound scales with dimension or sample size without a concrete example. A natural choice would be a simple nonlinear mixture model, such as polynomial or trigonometric mixing functions, where the Rademacher complexity of the encoder class can be explicitly calculated. Providing this computation would allow practitioners to calibrate the required sample size for their specific architecture and verify that the O(N^{-1/3}) rate is not overly pessimistic.

- Simulation demonstrating the regularizer under finite samples: The theoretical claims regarding private component identification rely on enforcing exact statistical independence, yet the implementation section only provides a minimax formulation without analyzing its finite-sample behavior. The paper lacks a simulation that explicitly tests how the regularizer performs when the independence assumption is partially violated or when the sample size is small. Running a controlled experiment on a synthetic dataset with known ground-truth mixing functions would show whether the proposed regularizer successfully isolates the private components or collapses into trivial solutions. Reporting standard disentanglement metrics alongside the generation examples would ground the theoretical guarantees in measurable performance.

- Guidance on enforcing approximate invertibility in practice: The identifiability proofs assume exact invertibility of both the data generating process and the learned encoders, but the practical implementation substitutes this hard constraint with a reconstruction loss. The paper does not discuss how to quantify the invertibility error or how non-invertible mappings might degrade the theoretical guarantees. Adding a section that details how to monitor Jacobian conditioning during training, or how to incorporate flow-based architectures to guarantee invertibility, would complete the implementation guidance. Explaining the trade-off between reconstruction accuracy and invertibility would also help practitioners tune the hyperparameters effectively.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical claims rest on strict assumptions that are not met by the proposed optimization strategy, the sample complexity rate lacks justification, and the empirical validation relies on qualitative results without standard disentanglement metrics. Addressing these gaps is necessary to establish the paper's theoretical rigor and practical relevance.

Revision targets:

- Clarify the derivation of the $2/3$ rate in Theorem 3 and explicitly link the derivative metric to latent recovery error or provide a simulation demonstrating sample complexity effects.

- Add an ablation study on the independence regularizer weight, report optimization stability metrics, and discuss how the regularizer interacts with the correlation objective.

- Incorporate explicit invertibility constraints or analyze the impact of reconstruction-based approximation on the identifiability bounds in Theorems 1 and 2.

- Acknowledge the prior finite-sample work by the same authors and refine the novelty claim to accurately distinguish this contribution.

- Replace qualitative generation examples with quantitative disentanglement metrics and compare against established independence-promoting baselines.

comments.title: Optimization sign error contradicts independence goal
comments.quote: \min_{\bm{U},\bm{\theta}}\max_{\bm{\eta}}\ \mathcal{L}+\beta\mathcal{V}+\lambda\mathcal{R},\quad\text{s.t.}\ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\ell=1}^{N}\bm{u}_{\ell}\bm{u}_{\ell}^{\top}=\bm{I},\ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\ell=1}^{N}\bm{u}_{\ell}=\bm{0},
comments.explanation: The objective maximizes $\mathcal{R}$ with respect to $\bm{\eta}$, but $\mathcal{R}$ measures correlation between shared and private components. Maximizing this term actively increases dependence, directly contradicting the goal of enforcing statistical independence. The adversarial setup should minimize $\mathcal{R}$ or negate it inside the maximization. Change the objective to $\min_{\bm{U},\bm{\theta}}\max_{\bm{\eta}}\ \mathcal{L}+\beta\mathcal{V}-\lambda\mathcal{R}$ to correctly penalize correlation.

comments.title: Undefined constant C_f in sample complexity bound
comments.quote:
where $\kappa_{j}=\Omega((
icefrac{{3}}{{C_{d}}})^{1/3}(4C_{f}(2D\mathfrak{R}_{N}+C_{f}\sqrt{
icefrac{{\log(1/\delta)}}{2N}})+4
u^{2})^{1/6})$
comments.explanation: The constant $C_f$ appears in the definition of $\kappa_j$ but is never introduced in Assumption 2 or the surrounding text. Assumption 2(d) defines bounds $C_d$ and $C_p$, but $C_f$ remains undefined. Without this definition, the derivation of the boundary region $\kappa_j$ cannot be verified, and the logical chain from the assumptions to the sample complexity bound is broken. Define $C_f$ explicitly or replace it with the correct constant from the derivation.

comments.title: Dimension constraint for invertible mixing function
comments.quote: $\bm{g}^{(q)}(\cdot):\mathbb{R}^{D+D_{q}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{M_{q}}$ is an *invertible* and *smooth* nonlinear transformation, which is unknown.
comments.explanation: A strictly invertible (bijective) map between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces requires equal domain and codomain dimensions. Without $M_q = D+D_q$, the function cannot be bijective, which breaks the change-of-variables arguments used in the identifiability proofs. The authors should explicitly state the dimension equality or clarify if 'invertible' is intended to mean 'injective with a left inverse'. Rewrite the sentence to specify $M_q = D+D_q$ to match the mathematical requirements for the subsequent derivations.

comments.title: BYOL distilled loss omits prediction head
comments.quote:
lows:

$\underset{\bm{f}^{(1)},\bm{f}^{(2)}}{\text{minimize}}\ \mathbb{E}\left[\left\|\overline{\bm{f}}^{(1)}(\bm{x}^{(1)})-\overline{\bm{f}}^{(2)}(\bm{x}^{(2)})\right\|_{2}^{2}\right]$ (4)

<!-- PA
comments.explanation: The distilled loss for BYOL in equation (4) omits the prediction head $p(\cdot)$, which is a critical architectural component that prevents representation collapse. The original BYOL objective minimizes $\|\overline{p(\bm{f}^{(1)}(\bm{x}^{(1)}))} - \overline{\bm{f}}^{(2)}(\bm{x}^{(2)})\|^2$. Removing this head changes the optimization landscape entirely and makes the simplified objective equivalent to standard cosine similarity minimization, which collapses without additional mechanisms. Add the prediction head to the first branch in equation (4) and explicitly state that it is the key mechanism preventing collapse.

comments.title: Asymptotic rate claim overlooks approximation error
comments.quote: The theorem indicates that with $N$ samples, the metric is bounded by $O(N^{-\nicefrac{{1}}{{3}}})$.
comments.explanation:
The bound in equation (8) is $O((D\mathfrak{R}_N + \sqrt{\log(1/\delta)/N} + 
u^2)^{2/3})$. While the Rademacher complexity and confidence terms decay as $O(N^{-1/2})$, the approximation error $
u^2$ is independent of the sample size $N$. Consequently, as $N 	o \infty$, the bound converges to $O(
u^{4/3})$ rather than vanishing. Claiming the metric is bounded by $O(N^{-1/3})$ without qualifying the role of $
u$ is mathematically imprecise. Rewrite the claim to reflect the full asymptotic behavior, e.g., 'The metric is bounded by $O(
u^{4/3}) + O(N^{-1/3})$, indicating that convergence to zero requires both $N 	o \infty$ and a sufficiently expressive function class to minimize $
u$.'

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of DCCA theoretical literature
comments.quote: Such properties were also observed in DCCA research *(Wang et al., 2015)*, while theoretical analysis is mostly elusive.
comments.explanation: Describing the theoretical analysis of DCCA as 'mostly elusive' is factually incorrect and overlooks significant recent progress. Several works have established identifiability guarantees for nonlinear CCA and DCCA, and finite-sample convergence bounds have been derived. The literature now contains rigorous conditions under which DCCA recovers shared latent components. The authors should update this statement to accurately reflect the existing theoretical landscape and clarify how their identifiability perspective builds upon or differs from these prior guarantees.

comments.title: Misattribution of theoretical support for private interference robustness
comments.quote: CCA was found particularly useful in fending against unknown and strong view-specific (private) interference (see theoretical supports in *(Bach and Jordan, 2005; Ibrahim and Sidiropoulos, 2020)*).
comments.explanation: The cited references do not substantiate this claim. Bach and Jordan (2005) presents a probabilistic interpretation of CCA assuming Gaussian latents, focusing on latent variable modeling rather than robustness to private interference. Ibrahim and Sidiropoulos (2020) establishes identifiability conditions for nonlinear CCA, not the method's filtering capabilities. The observation that CCA isolates shared components is a direct mathematical consequence of maximizing cross-covariance. The sentence should be revised to attribute this property to the fundamental definition of correlation maximization, and the citations should be removed or replaced with literature that explicitly analyzes robustness to view-specific noise.

comments.title: Abstract omits theoretical prerequisites
comments.quote: Under this model, latent correlation maximization is shown to guarantee the extraction of the shared components across views (up to certain ambiguities).
comments.explanation: The abstract claims a guarantee for shared component extraction but omits the critical assumptions required to prove it. The main text explicitly relies on the statistical independence between shared and private components (Assumption 1) and invertibility constraints on the mixing and encoding functions (Eq. 7b) to establish this result. Without mentioning these prerequisites, the abstract presents the identifiability claim as universally valid under the generative model. Rewrite the sentence to specify that the guarantee holds under the stated independence and invertibility conditions.

comments.title: Vague description of finite-sample analysis contribution
comments.quote: A finite sample analysis, which has been rare in nonlinear mixture identifiability study, is also presented.
comments.explanation: This sentence introduces the finite-sample analysis but provides no information about its main finding or practical implication. Readers expect an abstract to summarize the key theoretical takeaway, such as the convergence rate of the estimator or the bound on the identification error. Stating only that the analysis is rare and presented leaves the contribution vague. Rewrite this to explicitly state the derived bound and clarify what quantity it constrains, so the theoretical contribution is immediately clear.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding minimax regularizer implementation
comments.quote: The regularizer shares the same purpose of some known independence promoters (e.g., *Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion* (HSIC) *(Gretton et al., 2007)*) but is arguably easier to implement using stochastic gradient algorithms.
comments.explanation: HSIC is already standard and straightforward to implement with stochastic gradient algorithms using mini-batch estimates. The proposed minimax formulation typically introduces additional hyperparameters and optimization instability compared to direct HSIC minimization. The claim that the new regularizer is easier to implement is not supported by standard practice. Remove the comparative ease-of-implementation claim or provide a concrete justification for why the minimax approach reduces implementation complexity over HSIC.

comments.title: Strong independence assumption in latent modeling
comments.explanation: Assumption 1 enforces full mutual independence among the shared component and both private components. While the authors justify this for specific AM-SSL and DCCA scenarios, many multiview identifiability proofs only require conditional independence or pairwise independence. If the subsequent theorems rely on the full factorization in (6), the authors should explicitly state this dependency in the theorem conditions. Add a sentence after Assumption 1 noting that the subsequent identifiability proofs explicitly require this full independence structure, or clarify if weaker conditions suffice.

comments.title: Dangling quantifier in Theorem 3 sample condition
comments.explanation: The phrase 'and all $i\in[D]$' is syntactically dangling within the condition for $\bm{c}^{(q)}$. The inequality only involves indices $j \in [D_q]$ corresponding to the private components, and contains no reference to $i \in [D]$. This dangling quantifier creates ambiguity about whether the boundary condition on $\bm{c}^{(q)}$ was intended to depend on the shared component dimension. Remove 'and all $i\in[D]$' from the condition on $\bm{c}^{(q)}$, or relocate it to properly scope the derivative metric in equation (8).

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel theoretical framework linking latent correlation maximization in DCCA and AM-SSL to nonlinear mixture identifiability. The generative model and the proposed minimax regularizer are conceptually interesting, and the finite sample analysis attempts to address a significant gap in the literature. However, the theoretical foundations are not fully established, and the experimental validation does not rigorously test the identifiability claims.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Justification for Shared Component Identification is Missing: Theorem 1 claims that maximizing correlation identifies shared components up to an invertible function, but the text provides only an intuitive explanation rather than a rigorous proof. The paper states 'Theorem 1 clearly indicates...' without showing the derivation. The logic relies on the equivalence of the correlation objective to matching, but it does not rigorously show that maximizing trace of covariance forces the solution to correspond to the inverse of the mixing functions, especially when $g^{(1)}$ and $g^{(2)}$ are different. This gap undermines the core identifiability claim of the paper.

- Demonstration of Finite Sample Convergence Rate: The paper presents a finite sample analysis (Theorem 3) claiming a convergence rate of $O(N^{-1/3})$ for the disentanglement metric. However, the text lacks a concrete demonstration that this rate is achievable in practice. The authors should run a simulation where they vary the sample size $N$ and plot the empirical error against the theoretical bound. Without this, readers cannot verify if the theoretical result is non-vacuous or if the constants involved are realistic for deep neural networks.

- Worked Example for Private Component Disentanglement: While the paper validates shared component extraction on MNIST and CIFAR10, it does not provide a fully worked parametric example for the private component disentanglement claim (Theorem 2). The Cars3D experiment is described but lacks a quantitative analysis, such as computing the mutual information or reconstruction error between the extracted private components and the ground-truth private factors. A simple synthetic example (e.g., mixing Gaussian variables with different nonlinearities) would help readers calibrate the intuition that the proposed regularizer successfully separates private information.

- Comparison with Existing Disentanglement Methods: The paper introduces a novel minimax neural regularizer $ho$ for group independence. It claims this is easier to implement than HSIC but never formally compares its performance against standard baselines like MINE or InfoNCE on a standard disentanglement benchmark (e.g., dSprites or ATOMIC). The reader needs to see whether the proposed method offers a practical advantage in terms of disentanglement quality or optimization stability compared to the established literature.

- Implementation Guidance for Minimax Regularizer: The paper proposes a minimax formulation (10) involving a regularization term $ho$ defined via a supremum over neural networks. While it mentions using two neural networks for $ho$, it provides no details on the architecture, initialization, or training schedule for these networks. Given the complexity of a minimax setup, the paper is missing critical practical guidance that would allow other researchers to reproduce the results and understand the trade-offs involved in this design choice.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a complete proof for Theorem 1 demonstrating that maximizing correlation forces the solution to correspond to the inverse of the mixing functions.

- Clarify the derivation of the finite sample bound in Theorem 3, as the current explanation is opaque and the $O(N^{-1/3})$ rate seems optimistic.

- Add quantitative experiments that directly measure the alignment between the extracted shared components and the ground-truth shared factors, rather than relying solely on clustering accuracy.

comments.title: Missing Quantifier in Abstract
comments.quote: A finite sample analysis, which has been rare in nonlinear mixture identifiability study, is also presented.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims a finite sample analysis is presented but does not quantify the convergence rate, leaving readers without concrete expectations.

comments.title: Ambiguous Link Between Viewpoint and Tasks
comments.quote: In this work, we offer an understanding to the role of latent correlation maximization that is seen in a number of DCCA and AM-SSL systems from a nonlinear mixture learning viewpoint—and use such understanding to assist various learning tasks, e.g., clustering, cross-view translation, and cross-sample generation.
comments.explanation: The text implies that the nonlinear mixture viewpoint directly enables these tasks, but it does not specify how the theoretical understanding of shared component identification translates into practical benefits. Add a brief explanation of the mechanism, such as 'By identifying shared components, we obtain representations that are invariant to view-specific distortions, which facilitates clustering by reducing intra-class variance and enables cross-view generation by disentangling private factors.'

comments.title: Incorrect Mathematical Equivalence
comments.quote:
An equivalent representation of (1) is as follows

\[ \underset{\bm{f}^{(1)},\bm{f}^{(2)}}{\text{minimize}}\ \mathbb{E}\left[\left\|\bm{f}^{(1)}(\bm{x}^{(1)})-\bm{f}^{(2)}(\bm{x}^{(2)})\right\|_{2}^{2}\right],\quad\text{s.t.}\ \mathbb{E}\left[\bm{f}^{(q)}\left(\bm{x}^{(q)}\right)\bm{f}^{(q)}\left(\bm{x}^{(q)}\right)^{\top}\right]=\bm{I}, \] (2)
comments.explanation: The equivalence claimed between the correlation maximization form (1) and the component matching form (2) is mathematically incorrect. The correlation maximization objective maximizes the trace of the cross-covariance matrix, while the component matching objective minimizes the expected squared Euclidean distance between the two embeddings. These are not equivalent objectives. The equivalence holds only under specific conditions (e.g., when the embeddings are constrained to be orthonormal and have zero mean), which are not stated in the text.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Nonlinear ICA
comments.quote: al., 2020)*. The nICA works aim to separate nonlinearly mixed latent components to an individual component level, which is in general impossible unless additional information associated with each sample (e.g., time frame labels *(Hyvarinen
comments.explanation: The paper claims nonlinear ICA is 'impossible' without additional labels, which misrepresents the field. Nonlinear ICA is a well-established field with numerous algorithms (e.g., infomax, contrastive, score-based) that aim to separate nonlinear mixtures without sample-specific labels. While identifiability is indeed a major challenge and often requires constraints (orthogonality, independence, or specific distributions), it is not strictly impossible. The cited works provide rigorous identifiability results under specific conditions, not proofs of impossibility.

comments.title: Misleading Comparison with Von Kügelgen et al.
comments.quote: . A key difference is that their model uses an identical nonlinear generative function across the views (i.e.,  $\pmb{g}^{(1)} = \pmb{g}^{(2)}$ ), while we consider two possibly different
comments.explanation: The comparison with Von Kügelgen et al. is misleading because it ignores their focus on content-style disentanglement. The distinction between 'identical' and 'possibly different' functions is not a unique novelty of the current work but rather a standard assumption in the field. Furthermore, the paper's own Theorem 1 explicitly handles the case of different g^(q), suggesting this is a generalization of standard results rather than a radical departure from prior work.

comments.title: Factual Error Regarding Private Component Extraction
comments.quote: 's. In addition, our learning criterion is able to extract the private information, while the work in (Von Kugelgen et al., 2021) did not consider this asp
comments.explanation: The statement that Von Kügelgen et al. did not consider private information extraction is likely incorrect. Their work focuses on 'content-style disentanglement,' which inherently involves separating style (private) from content (shared) information. While their specific objective or identifiability conditions might differ from the current work, the claim that they 'did not consider this aspect' is a factual error that misrepresents the content of their paper. The current work should be positioned as offering a *different* or *more general* approach to disentanglement, not as the first to address it.

comments.title: Order Inconsistency in Experimental Section
comments.quote: Synthetic Data. We first use synthetic data for theory validation; see the supplementary materials.
comments.explanation: The experimental section lists subsections 6.1 and 6.2 before the 'Synthetic Data' header, yet the text claims to start with synthetic data. This creates a contradiction regarding the order of presentation.

comments.title: Ambiguous Description of Cars3D Experiment
comments.quote: For example, on the left of Fig. 2, the  $z_{\ell}^{(1)}$  is extracted from the red convertible, and the  $\tilde{c}_j^{(1)}$ 's are extracted from the top row. One can see that the generated  $\tilde{\pmb{x}}_{\ell,j}^{(1)}$ 's under the proposed method with  $\mathcal{R}$  are all red convertibles with the same elevation, but using the azimuths of the corresponding cars from the top row.
comments.explanation: The description of the Cars3D experiment is ambiguous regarding whether the azimuths in the top row refer to the ground-truth private factors or the extracted components. This ambiguity makes it difficult to verify whether the method successfully disentangles the private components from the shared components.

comments.title: Implication of Theorem 1 Requires Explicit Justification
comments.quote: In particular, we modeled multiview data as nonlinear mixtures of shared and private components, and showed that latent correlation maximization ensures to extract the shared components—which are believed to be identity-revealing.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that latent correlation maximization 'ensures to extract the shared components' without explicitly referencing the conditions of Theorem 1. Readers need to know which assumptions from Section 3.3 are necessary for this implication to be valid.

comments.title: Sample Complexity Claim Needs Cross-Reference
comments.quote: We also analyzed the sample complexity for extracting the shared information, which has not been addressed in nonlinear component analysis works, to our best knowledge.
comments.explanation: The conclusion mentions sample complexity analysis but fails to reference Theorem 3, making it difficult for readers to locate the specific result.

comments.title: Regularizer Design Lacks Practical Guidance
comments.quote: To realize our learning criterion, we proposed a slack variable-assisted latent correlation maximization approach, with a novel minimax neural regularizer for promoting group independence.
comments.explanation: The conclusion introduces a 'novel minimax neural regularizer' but offers no practical guidance on its architecture or training, which is necessary for reproducibility.
